title: Judge approves Anthropic's $1.5B copyright settlement
summary: A federal judge granted final approval to the largest US copyright settlement on record, covering about 500,000 books Anthropic downloaded from pirate libraries.
body:
A federal judge in San Francisco has granted final approval to Anthropic's $1.5 billion settlement with book authors, closing the largest copyright settlement in US history and setting a reference price for training data taken from pirate libraries.

## The terms

US District Judge Araceli Martínez-Olguín signed off on the deal this week, overruling objections from authors who argued the amount was too low, that attorneys were overcompensated, or that some rights holders were wrongly excluded. The settlement covers roughly 500,000 works at an expected payout of about $3,000 per title. Participation is strikingly high: about 91% of eligible authors filed claims, and reporting puts opt-outs at only around 350 — leaving very few plaintiffs positioned to pursue separate suits.

## How the case got here

The class action, brought by three authors in 2024 (Bartz v. Anthropic), produced one of the most consequential rulings in AI law: then-Judge William Alsup found that training on lawfully purchased books qualifies as fair use, while downloading and retaining millions of pirated copies from shadow libraries does not. That split ruling exposed Anthropic to statutory damages that could theoretically have reached hundreds of billions of dollars, pushing it to settle in 2025. Alsup has since retired, leaving Martínez-Olguín to complete the approval.

## Why it matters

The approval converts a theoretical legal risk into a concrete market signal: acquisition method, not training itself, is where US copyright liability currently bites, and the going rate for a pirated book is about $3,000. Every AI lab facing similar claims — and every plaintiff's lawyer drafting them, including in the pending Sony-Udio music dispute — now has a benchmark. For authors, the near-universal claims rate suggests creators would rather take structured compensation than gamble on litigation, a dynamic that may accelerate licensing deals as the cheaper path for labs going forward.
title_zh: 15 亿美元和解案终获批准,盗版书训练 AI 有了定价
summary_zh: 旧金山联邦法官正式批准 Anthropic 与作家群体的 15 亿美元和解,覆盖约 50 万部被从盗版库下载的作品,每部赔付约 3000 美元,创美国版权和解纪录。
body_zh:
旧金山联邦法院本周对 Anthropic 与图书作者的 15 亿美元和解案作出最终批准,美国历史上金额最大的版权和解就此落槌,也为"从盗版库获取训练数据"标出了市场价。

## 和解条款

联邦地区法官 Araceli Martínez-Olguín 驳回了部分作者的异议——有人认为金额太低,有人质疑原告律师分成过高,还有人主张部分权利人被错误排除——最终签署批准令。和解覆盖约 50 万部作品,每部预计赔付约 3000 美元。参与率相当惊人:约 91% 的符合条件作者提交了索赔,选择退出者据报道仅 350 人左右,意味着几乎没有人保留另行起诉的空间。

## 案件来龙去脉

这起集体诉讼由三位作家于 2024 年发起(Bartz 诉 Anthropic 案),并催生了 AI 法律史上最具分量的裁决之一:时任法官 William Alsup 认定,用合法购买的图书训练模型属于合理使用,但从影子图书馆下载并留存数百万册盗版书不属于。这一"各打五十大板"的裁决让 Anthropic 面临理论上可达数千亿美元的法定赔偿风险,促使其在 2025 年选择和解。Alsup 此后退休,批准程序由 Martínez-Olguín 接手完成。

## 为什么重要

批准令把悬置的法律风险变成了明确的市场信号:在美国版权框架下,真正咬人的是数据获取方式而非训练行为本身,而一本盗版书的"行价"约为 3000 美元。所有面临类似指控的 AI 实验室——以及正在起草诉状的原告律师,包括进行中的索尼诉 Udio 音乐版权案——从此都有了参照系。对作者而言,接近全员的索赔率说明创作者更愿意拿确定的补偿而非赌诉讼结果,这种心态可能加速版权方与实验室之间的授权交易,使"先谈授权"成为更便宜的路径。

sources.title: TechCrunch: Anthropic's landmark $1.5B copyright settlement is approved
AP: Judge approves $1.5B Anthropic settlement over pirated books
Publishers Weekly: Judge gives final approval of $1.5 billion Anthropic settlement
